title: No Begging, No Bidding: How Nvidia's GPU Queue Actually Works

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
The mythology of GPU allocation—whispered stories of CEOs begging Jensen Huang at dinner for scarce chips—has become part of AI industry folklore. Huang pushes back hard on this narrative, insisting the process is far more mundane than the gossip suggests. The famous dinner with Larry Ellison and Elon Musk? "At no time did they beg for GPUs. They just had to place an order."

Nvidia's allocation system operates on a straightforward principle: first in, first out. Companies must submit purchase orders, and those who order earlier get served earlier. There's a secondary optimization layer—if a customer's data center isn't ready to receive shipments, Nvidia may serve another customer first to maximize factory throughput—but the core logic is queue-based, not auction-based. Huang is emphatic that Nvidia never adjusts pricing based on demand spikes.

This pricing discipline is a deliberate strategic choice. Huang frames it as building trust and dependability—"I prefer to be dependable, to be the foundation of the industry." He draws an explicit parallel to Nvidia's relationship with TSMC, which has operated without a formal legal contract for nearly 30 years on the basis of mutual trust and "rough justice." The consistency of pricing and allocation is positioned as a competitive moat in itself, one that encourages long-term planning by customers.

The deeper insight is that Nvidia's allocation philosophy reflects its ecosystem-first strategy. By refusing to maximize short-term revenue through dynamic pricing, Nvidia ensures that smaller players—neoclouds, startups, research institutions—can plan around predictable costs. This broadens the install base, which in turn strengthens CUDA's network effects. The queue system isn't just fair; it's a flywheel mechanism disguised as an operational policy.
